agent_output: {"escalations":[{"type":"contradiction","description":"Bird requires leaderboard rankings to reflect score changes within 100ms with no stale reads. MJ's architecture routes all reads through a Fastly CDN cache with a 30-second TTL. Pippen confirms this means reads can be up to 30 seconds stale — a 300x violation of Bird's 100ms freshness rule. However, Pippen also confirms the CDN cache is a hard operational requirement to avoid overwhelming Redis at 50,000 concurrent viewers. These two requirements are directly incompatible: you cannot serve fresh-within-100ms data from a 30-second cache, but you cannot remove the cache without losing the ability to handle peak load.","agents_involved":["Bird","MJ","Pippen"],"routed_to":"Coach K","options":["Option A: Relax Bird's 100ms freshness rule to 30 seconds for the public leaderboard view, keeping 100ms only for the scoring player's own rank (read-your-own-writes via direct Redis query, bypassing CDN). This satisfies the operational constraint while giving the most important user — the one who just scored — immediate feedback.","Option B: Replace the 30s CDN TTL with a very short TTL (e.g., 100ms–500ms) or event-driven cache invalidation (Fastly Instant Purge on score events). This preserves Bird's freshness rule but requires validating that Redis + purge throughput can handle 50,000 viewers at near-zero cache hit rates during high-scoring periods.","Option C: Use WebSockets or Server-Sent Events to push leaderboard updates directly to connected viewers, bypassing CDN for the live data channel entirely. CDN serves the initial page load only. This satisfies both requirements but significantly changes the architecture.","Option D: Introduce a tiered model — top N ranks are pushed in real-time (WebSocket), remaining ranks served via CDN with 30s TTL. This balances operational load with freshness where it matters most."],"recommendation":"Option A is the lowest-risk resolution — it preserves operational safety and gives the scoring player immediate feedback. But Bird must explicitly approve relaxing the 100ms rule for passive viewers, since that is a domain authority decision. If Bird insists on 100ms for all viewers, Option C or D become necessary and MJ needs to redesign the read path."}],"confidence":{"level":35,"high_confidence_areas":["The contradiction is real and clearly surfaced by all three agents","Redis Sorted Sets are the correct data structure for the scoring path","CDN caching is operationally required at 50,000 concurrent viewers"],"low_confidence_areas":["Which resolution Bird will accept — this is a domain authority call","Whether Fastly Instant Purge throughput is sufficient for high-frequency score events","Whether a WebSocket approach changes the operational profile enough to satisfy Pippen"],"assumptions":["All 50,000 concurrent viewers are hitting the same leaderboard endpoint","Score events are frequent enough that a 30s window will almost always contain changes","The 100ms requirement applies to all readers, not just the scoring player (needs Bird confirmation)"]}}

content.thinking:
Let me analyze the inputs from Bird, MJ, and Pippen for contradictions.

Bird says: 100ms freshness, no stale reads
MJ says: 30s CDN cache TTL
Pippen says: 30s CDN TTL violates Bird's 100ms requirement, but CDN is operationally required

This is a clear contradiction between Bird's domain rules and MJ's architecture. Pippen has explicitly surfaced it. I must escalate and NOT produce a handoff brief.

content.text: {"escalations":[{"type":"contradiction","description":"Bird requires leaderboard rankings to reflect score changes within 100ms with no stale reads. MJ's architecture routes all reads through a Fastly CDN cache with a 30-second TTL. Pippen confirms this means reads can be up to 30 seconds stale — a 300x violation of Bird's 100ms freshness rule. However, Pippen also confirms the CDN cache is a hard operational requirement to avoid overwhelming Redis at 50,000 concurrent viewers. This preserves Bird's freshness rule but requires validating that Redis + purge throughput can handle 50,000 viewers at near-zero cache hit rates during high-scoring periods.","Option C: Use WebSockets or Server-Sent Events to push leaderboard updates directly to connected viewers, bypassing CDN for the live data channel entirely. CDN serves the initial page load only. This satisfies both requirements but significantly changes the architecture.","Option D: Introduce a tiered model — top N ranks are pushed in real-time (WebSocket), remaining ranks served via CDN with 30s TTL. But Bird must explicitly approve relaxing the 100ms rule for passive viewers, since that is a domain authority decision.
